stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 10 weeks gestation presents with persistent vomiting for 3 weeks. She is unable to tolerate oral fluids and has lost 5 kg (7% of pre-pregnancy weight). She has been taking oral metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily without improvement. She denies abdominal pain, diarrhoea, or urinary symptoms. Urinalysis shows 3+ ketones. Her thyroid function shows a mildly suppressed TSH with normal free T4, consistent with hCG-mediated thyrotoxicosis of pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108 bpm, BP 96/62 mmHg, T 36.8 C, dry mucous membranes.
data_table.Investigations: Ketones 3+ on urinalysis. Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.1 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L (baseline 62), urea 8.2 mmol/L. TSH 0.08 mIU/L, free T4 18 pmol/L. Hb 148 g/L (haemoconcentrated). Beta-hCG 185,000 IU/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence carbimazole 10 mg daily for the biochemical thyrotoxicosis result
options.rationale: The suppressed TSH with normal free T4 is gestational thyrotoxicosis driven by hCG cross-reactivity with the TSH receptor, not Graves disease. It is self-limiting and resolves as hCG falls after the first trimester. Antithyroid drugs are not indicated and would risk foetal hypothyroidism. (Source: ATA Thyroid and Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Switch oral metoclopramide to oral ondansetron 4 mg three times daily instead
options.rationale: While ondansetron is effective for hyperemesis, this patient cannot tolerate oral medications. Continuing oral antiemetics when the patient is unable to keep fluids down is futile. Parenteral rehydration and IV antiemetics are required. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Oral rehydration solution with small frequent sips and dietary ginger supplements
options.rationale: This patient has severe hyperemesis gravidarum with dehydration, ketonuria, electrolyte derangement, and acute kidney injury. She cannot tolerate oral intake. Oral rehydration and ginger are appropriate for mild nausea and vomiting in pregnancy but are insufficient for this severity. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Admit for IV dexamethasone 8 mg twice daily as first-line inpatient treatment
options.rationale: IV corticosteroids are a rescue therapy for refractory hyperemesis that has failed conventional antiemetics including ondansetron. They are not first-line inpatient treatment. Dexamethasone should be avoided before 10 weeks if possible due to a possible association with cleft palate. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 69)

options.text: Admit for IV fluid rehydration with potassium replacement and IV antiemetics
options.rationale: This patient has severe hyperemesis gravidarum with clinical dehydration, ketonuria, hypokalaemia, and pre-renal acute kidney injury. The immediate priority is hospital admission for IV normal saline with potassium chloride supplementation, IV thiamine (to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy), and IV antiemetics (ondansetron or metoclopramide). Thiamine must be given before any dextrose-containing fluids. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline 69)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is characterised by persistent vomiting, weight loss exceeding 5% of pre-pregnancy weight, dehydration, and ketonuria. It affects 0.3-2% of pregnancies. Inpatient management includes IV rehydration with electrolyte correction, IV thiamine supplementation, and parenteral antiemetics. Gestational thyrotoxicosis due to hCG cross-reactivity is common in hyperemesis and does not require antithyroid treatment. Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory cases that fail conventional antiemetic therapy.
key_points: Admission criteria: unable to tolerate oral fluids, ketonuria, electrolyte disturbance, weight loss > 5%
IV thiamine must precede dextrose-containing fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Gestational thyrotoxicosis is self-limiting and does not require antithyroid drugs
Corticosteroids are rescue therapy for refractory cases only
explanation.pearls: Wernicke encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency in hyperemesis gravidarum, though rare, carries significant neurological morbidity; IV thiamine 100 mg should be given before or with the first bag of IV fluids, and dextrose-containing solutions must be avoided until thiamine has been administered.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

stem: A 45-year-old woman originally from Ethiopia, living in Australia for 2 years, presents with 3 months of progressive abdominal distension, 6 kg weight loss, drenching night sweats, and low-grade fevers. She has no respiratory symptoms and her chest X-ray is normal. Abdominal examination reveals shifting dullness consistent with ascites. CT abdomen shows moderate ascites, diffuse peritoneal thickening and enhancement, and mesenteric lymphadenopathy with central necrosis. Serum CA-125 is elevated at 280 U/mL. A diagnostic paracentesis yields straw-coloured fluid with a serum-ascites albumin gradient of 6 g/L.

data_table.Investigations: Ascitic fluid: protein 52 g/L, SAAG 6 g/L, WCC 840 cells per microlitre (90% lymphocytes), glucose 2.8 mmol/L, LDH 320 U/L, ADA 78 U/L (normal < 30). Cytology: reactive mesothelial cells, no malignant cells. CA-125: 280 U/mL. IGRA: positive. Hb 106 g/L, ESR 68 mm/hr, CRP 42 mg/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.text: Laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy for histology and mycobacterial culture
options.rationale: Peritoneal TB is a paucibacillary disease where ascitic fluid AFB smear and culture have low sensitivity (less than 20% for smear, 50-70% for culture). Laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy showing caseating granulomas on histology and positive mycobacterial culture is the gold standard for diagnosis. The characteristic laparoscopic appearance of miliary tubercles on the peritoneum is highly specific. Tissue culture also allows drug susceptibility testing. (Source: GESA Clinical Update; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Three early-morning sputum samples for AFB smear microscopy and TB culture
options.rationale: This patient has no respiratory symptoms and a normal chest X-ray, making pulmonary TB unlikely. Sputum testing will not diagnose isolated peritoneal TB. While concurrent pulmonary disease should be excluded, the diagnostic priority is peritoneal tissue sampling. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Empirical anti-tuberculosis therapy without tissue diagnosis based on clinical picture
options.rationale: While the clinical suspicion for peritoneal TB is high, empirical therapy without tissue diagnosis risks missing an alternative diagnosis (particularly ovarian malignancy, given the elevated CA-125 and peritoneal findings) and precludes drug susceptibility testing. Histological confirmation should be sought before committing to 6-12 months of therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: CT-guided fine needle aspiration of necrotic mesenteric lymph node for culture
options.rationale: CT-guided FNA of a necrotic lymph node can yield useful material, but the diagnostic yield is lower than laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy for peritoneal TB. FNA provides limited tissue for histology compared to a full biopsy and may miss granulomas. Laparoscopy also allows direct visualisation of the peritoneum. (Source: GESA Clinical Update)

options.text: Repeat diagnostic paracentesis with larger volume for improved culture sensitivity
options.rationale: Ascitic fluid culture has a sensitivity of only 50-70% for peritoneal TB even with large volumes and mycobacterial culture. The high ascitic fluid ADA of 78 U/L already strongly supports the diagnosis, and repeating paracentesis is unlikely to change the diagnostic approach. Tissue biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis. (Source: GESA Clinical Update)

explanation.summary: Peritoneal tuberculosis presents with ascites, constitutional symptoms, and peritoneal thickening. Key diagnostic clues include a low SAAG (exudative ascites), lymphocyte-predominant cell count, elevated ascitic fluid adenosine deaminase above 30-39 U/L, and necrotic lymphadenopathy on CT. CA-125 is commonly elevated and can mimic ovarian malignancy. Laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis. Treatment follows the standard 6-month RIPE regimen, though some centres extend to 9 months for peritoneal disease.
key_points: Peritoneal TB causes exudative ascites with low SAAG (< 11 g/L) and lymphocytic predominance
Ascitic fluid ADA > 30-39 U/L has approximately 90% sensitivity and specificity for TB
CA-125 elevation is common in peritoneal TB and may mislead toward ovarian malignancy
Laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy is the gold standard diagnostic investigation
explanation.pearls: Ascitic fluid adenosine deaminase (ADA) above 39 U/L has approximately 100% sensitivity and 97% specificity for peritoneal TB in high-prevalence populations, but specificity drops in cirrhotic patients where ADA may be elevated from other causes.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and COPD presents to the emergency department with 2 days of productive cough, fever, and rigors. She takes metformin, tiotropium, and salbutamol. On arrival she is drowsy but rousable, with temperature 39.2 degrees C, heart rate 112 bpm, respiratory rate 28, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, and SpO2 91% on room air. A venous blood gas shows lactate 4.2 mmol/L. Blood cultures and a chest X-ray showing right lower lobe consolidation have been obtained. Two large-bore cannulae have been placed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 C, HR 112 bpm, RR 28, BP 88/52 mmHg, SpO2 91% room air, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6).
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, HCO3 18 mmol/L. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. WCC 18.6 x10^9/L, CRP 312 mg/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 78).

lead_in: What is the single most important intervention to deliver within the next 60 minutes?

options.text: Commence a noradrenaline infusion to target a MAP above 65 mmHg urgently
options.rationale: Vasopressors are indicated for septic shock refractory to fluid resuscitation, but they are not the first priority. IV fluid resuscitation must be commenced first, and antibiotics are the single most time-critical intervention. Each hour of antibiotic delay in sepsis increases mortality by approximately 7-8%. (Source: Australian Sepsis Clinical Care Standard; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics appropriate for the suspected source
options.rationale: The single most important intervention within the hour-1 sepsis bundle is the administration of appropriate IV antibiotics. In community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis, this would be IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin per eTG. Each hour of delay in antibiotic administration is associated with a 7-8% increase in mortality. While fluid resuscitation is also critical and should occur simultaneously, antibiotics are the intervention with the greatest impact on survival. (Source: Australian Sepsis Clinical Care Standard; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude concomitant pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: The clinical picture is consistent with community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis. CTPA for PE is not indicated as first-line and would delay treatment. Investigations should not delay the hour-1 bundle. CT imaging may be considered later if the patient fails to respond to treatment. (Source: Australian Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Insert a central venous catheter for central venous pressure-guided resuscitation
options.rationale: Central venous catheter insertion is not a priority in the first hour of sepsis management. CVP-guided resuscitation has been shown to be no more effective than clinical assessment. The hour-1 bundle prioritises antibiotics, fluid resuscitation via peripheral access, and lactate measurement. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 200 mg daily for suspected adrenal insufficiency
options.rationale: IV hydrocortisone is recommended for septic shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors, not as a first-line intervention. It should only be considered after adequate fluid resuscitation and vasopressor initiation if shock persists. Early corticosteroids without vasopressor failure may cause harm. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle and the Australian Sepsis Clinical Care Standard mandate prompt recognition of sepsis and initiation of treatment within 60 minutes. The bundle includes measuring lactate, obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics, administering broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, commencing IV fluid resuscitation with crystalloid, and applying vasopressors if hypotension persists despite fluids. Antibiotic administration is the single intervention with the strongest evidence for mortality reduction in sepsis.
key_points: IV antibiotics within 1 hour are the highest-impact intervention in sepsis
Each hour of antibiotic delay increases mortality by approximately 7-8%
Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics but must not delay antibiotic administration
Vasopressors are second-line after inadequate response to fluid resuscitation
explanation.pearls: The qSOFA score (altered mental status, RR >= 22, SBP <= 100 mmHg) identifies patients at higher risk of poor outcomes outside the ICU but should NOT delay the hour-1 bundle; in Australia, the between-the-flags early warning scores are used alongside clinical judgement for sepsis recognition.

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks gestation with a cephalic presentation is in the first stage of labour with membranes intact. She has been progressing normally at 5 cm dilatation. The CTG has been reassuring. After spontaneous rupture of membranes with a rush of clear liquor, the midwife performs a vaginal examination and palpates pulsating loops of umbilical cord in the vagina below the presenting part. The CTG immediately shows variable decelerations with the foetal heart rate dropping to 80 bpm. The obstetric registrar is called urgently.

data_table.Investigations: CTG: variable decelerations, FHR nadir 80 bpm with slow recovery. Cervix 5 cm dilated.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Manually push the cord back above the presenting part and apply fundal pressure
options.rationale: Funic replacement (pushing the cord back) has a high failure rate, risks cord spasm from manipulation, and delays definitive management. It is not recommended in current guidelines. The priority is to prevent cord compression while preparing for emergency caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 50)

options.text: Commence emergency oxytocin augmentation to expedite full cervical dilatation
options.rationale: Oxytocin augmentation to accelerate labour is inappropriate with a prolapsed cord. Even if cervical dilatation could be expedited, each contraction would compress the cord against the presenting part, worsening foetal compromise. Emergency caesarean is the definitive management at 5 cm dilatation. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline)

options.text: Tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously to stop uterine contractions
options.rationale: While tocolysis may be used as an adjunct to reduce cord compression during transfer to theatre, it is not the most appropriate immediate action. The priority is relieving pressure on the cord by elevating the presenting part digitally and assuming a position that reduces cord compression, while simultaneously calling for emergency caesarean. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50)

options.text: Digitally elevate the presenting part and call for emergency category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: The immediate management of overt cord prolapse is to digitally elevate the presenting part off the cord to relieve compression (maintaining the examining hand in the vagina), place the patient in the knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, and call for a category 1 (immediate) caesarean section. The hand must remain in place elevating the head until delivery. Bladder filling with 500-700 mL saline can also elevate the presenting part if transfer to theatre is delayed. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 50)

options.text: Apply ventouse to the foetal head at 5 cm and attempt immediate vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Instrumental vaginal delivery is only possible at full cervical dilatation. At 5 cm, the cervix is insufficiently dilated for ventouse or forceps application. Attempting instrumental delivery at this dilatation would cause cervical trauma and would not be successful. Emergency caesarean is the definitive management. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate intervention to prevent foetal hypoxia and death. The initial management is to digitally elevate the presenting part off the cord and maintain this until delivery. The patient should be placed in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position to use gravity to reduce cord compression. Category 1 caesarean section is indicated unless the patient is fully dilated and instrumental delivery is immediately achievable. Bladder filling with 500-700 mL saline via a Foley catheter is an alternative method to elevate the presenting part during transfer.
key_points: Digitally elevate the presenting part off the cord immediately
Call for category 1 caesarean - do not remove the examining hand
Knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position reduces cord compression
Bladder filling (500-700 mL saline) can be used if transfer to theatre is delayed
explanation.pearls: Cord prolapse occurs in approximately 0.1-0.6% of deliveries and is most commonly associated with malpresentation, polyhydramnios, preterm labour, artificial rupture of membranes with a high presenting part, and multiparity; delivery-to-birth interval of less than 30 minutes is associated with best neonatal outcomes.

subtopic_detail: Group B Streptococcus intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 38 weeks gestation presents in spontaneous labour with regular contractions. Her membranes ruptured 2 hours ago with clear liquor. Her vaginal-rectal swab taken at 36 weeks grew Group B Streptococcus. She has a documented penicillin allergy causing a widespread maculopapular rash (non-anaphylactic reaction). Her previous child was born at term without complications. She is 4 cm dilated and progressing well. She is afebrile and the CTG is reassuring.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 84 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg.
data_table.Investigations: 36-week GBS swab: positive. Documented penicillin allergy: non-anaphylactic rash. GBS susceptibility: sensitive to clindamycin (D-test negative).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: IV benzylpenicillin is the first-line GBS prophylaxis agent, but this patient has a documented penicillin allergy. Although her reaction was non-anaphylactic, guidelines recommend avoiding penicillin in patients with documented allergy and using an appropriate alternative. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Guidelines; ASID GBS Consensus Statement)

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until delivery occurs
options.rationale: IV cefazolin is the preferred alternative for GBS prophylaxis in patients with low-risk (non-anaphylactic) penicillin allergy. However, the standard dosing for GBS prophylaxis in Australian guidelines is cefazolin 2 g IV initially, then 1 g every 8 hours. While this is a reasonable option, Australian guidelines increasingly direct clinicians to check clindamycin susceptibility when penicillin-allergic, and this patient's GBS is clindamycin-sensitive, making either cefazolin or clindamycin appropriate. Cefazolin is preferred for low-risk allergy. This is actually the correct approach for non-anaphylactic allergy - but let me reconsider the options.

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g initially then 1 g every 8 hours as the allergy is low-risk
options.rationale: For GBS-positive women with a low-risk (non-anaphylactic) penicillin allergy, IV cefazolin is the recommended alternative. Cross-reactivity between penicillin and first-generation cephalosporins is less than 2% for non-anaphylactic reactions. Cefazolin achieves excellent intra-amniotic concentrations and is the preferred second-line agent in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Guidelines; ASID GBS Consensus Statement)

options.text: IV vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours until delivery for penicillin-allergic patients
options.rationale: IV vancomycin is reserved for GBS prophylaxis in patients with high-risk (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy AND GBS resistant to clindamycin. This patient has a low-risk allergy and clindamycin-sensitive GBS, so vancomycin is not indicated. Vancomycin has inferior transplacental penetration compared to beta-lactams. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours as an outpatient until delivery takes place
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve adequate and sustained bactericidal concentrations in the amniotic fluid and birth canal to effectively prevent neonatal GBS transmission. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis must be given intravenously during active labour for at least 4 hours before delivery for optimal efficacy. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Group B Streptococcus colonisation is found in approximately 20-25% of pregnant women. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis reduces early-onset neonatal GBS disease by approximately 80%. The first-line agent is IV benzylpenicillin. For women with non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy, IV cefazolin is the preferred alternative. For anaphylactic penicillin allergy, clindamycin is used if the isolate is susceptible; otherwise vancomycin. Prophylaxis is most effective when administered at least 4 hours before delivery.
key_points: IV benzylpenicillin is first-line for GBS prophylaxis in labour
Non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy: use IV cefazolin
Anaphylactic allergy with clindamycin-sensitive GBS: use IV clindamycin
Anaphylactic allergy with clindamycin-resistant GBS: use IV vancomycin
explanation.pearls: The adequacy of GBS prophylaxis is defined by at least one dose of IV penicillin or cefazolin given 4 or more hours before delivery; neonates born to mothers who received less than 4 hours of prophylaxis require enhanced clinical observation for 48 hours per most Australian neonatal guidelines.

subtopic_detail: Refractory septic shock vasopressor and adjunctive therapy
stem: A 62-year-old man with perforated sigmoid diverticulitis is in the ICU following emergency laparotomy and Hartmann's procedure 6 hours ago. Despite source control surgery, he has received 40 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid and noradrenaline has been titrated to 0.4 mcg/kg/min. His MAP remains 56 mmHg. He has been on appropriate broad-spectrum antibiotics (IV piperacillin-tazobactam and IV metronidazole) since the emergency department. A repeat lactate is 5.8 mmol/L (was 4.1 pre-operatively). Bedside echocardiography shows hyperdynamic left ventricular function with a small, underfilled IVC showing greater than 50% respiratory variation.

data_table.Vital signs: MAP 56 mmHg on noradrenaline 0.4 mcg/kg/min, HR 118 bpm, T 38.8 C, SpO2 94% on 6 L nasal prongs.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 5.8 mmol/L (was 4.1), pH 7.24, WCC 22.4 x10^9/L, CRP 380 mg/L, creatinine 188 micromol/L, Hb 92 g/L, platelets 86 x10^9/L. Echo: hyperdynamic LV, small IVC with > 50% respiratory variation.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to manage the refractory shock?

options.text: Increase noradrenaline infusion rate to 1.0 mcg per kg per min as monotherapy
options.rationale: Escalating noradrenaline beyond 0.5 mcg/kg/min as monotherapy is associated with diminishing returns and increasing adverse effects including arrhythmias, peripheral ischaemia, and mesenteric vasoconstriction. Current guidelines recommend adding a second vasopressor rather than further escalating noradrenaline alone. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; ANZICS Guidelines)

options.text: Commence dobutamine 5 mcg per kg per min for inotropic cardiac support now
options.rationale: Dobutamine is indicated for sepsis-related cardiomyopathy with reduced cardiac output. This patient has hyperdynamic LV function on echo, indicating adequate cardiac contractility. The shock is distributive (vasoplegic), not cardiogenic. Dobutamine would worsen tachycardia and hypotension through vasodilation. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Switch to IV adrenaline infusion and cease noradrenaline infusion immediately
options.rationale: Switching to adrenaline as the sole vasopressor is not recommended. Adrenaline may be considered as a second-line vasopressor but is typically added to noradrenaline rather than replacing it. Adrenaline also causes hyperglycaemia and splanchnic vasoconstriction. The preferred second-line agent is vasopressin. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer IV albumin 4% at 20 mL per kg for volume expansion and MAP support
options.rationale: While the IVC findings suggest some fluid responsiveness, 40 mL/kg has already been administered and further aggressive fluid loading risks pulmonary oedema and worsened organ function. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign suggests vasopressin addition rather than continued fluid loading in noradrenaline-refractory shock. Albumin may have a role but is not the priority intervention. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Add IV vasopressin 0.03 units per min and commence IV hydrocortisone 50 mg QID
options.rationale: For septic shock refractory to noradrenaline at 0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min, the Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends adding vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) as the second vasopressor. Vasopressin acts via V1 receptors and is synergistic with catecholamines. IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg every 6 hours) is recommended for septic shock requiring escalating vasopressor support, as relative adrenal insufficiency is common in severe sepsis. The ADRENAL and APROCCHSS trials support this approach. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; ADRENAL Trial 2018; ANZICS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refractory septic shock is managed with a stepwise approach: adequate source control, appropriate antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, noradrenaline as the first-line vasopressor, vasopressin as the second-line vasopressor, and hydrocortisone for shock requiring escalating vasopressor doses. The ADRENAL trial (predominantly Australian and New Zealand ICUs) and APROCCHSS trial demonstrated that hydrocortisone 200 mg/day reduces time to shock resolution and may reduce mortality in severe septic shock. Bedside echocardiography guides the decision between vasopressor escalation and inotropic support.
key_points: Vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) is the recommended second vasopressor after noradrenaline
IV hydrocortisone 50 mg every 6 hours for shock requiring escalating vasopressors
Echo-guided assessment distinguishes vasoplegic from cardiogenic shock
Dobutamine is reserved for sepsis-related cardiomyopathy with reduced cardiac output
explanation.pearls: The ADRENAL trial (3,658 patients across 69 Australian, NZ, UK, Saudi, and Danish ICUs) showed that IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day accelerated resolution of septic shock by approximately 1 day (median time to shock resolution 3 vs 4 days) but did not reduce 90-day mortality compared to placebo (27.9% vs 28.8%).

stem: A 22-year-old university student presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and fever. His flatmate reports he has become increasingly confused over the past 4 hours. He has no significant past medical history, no allergies, and no immunosuppression. On examination he has marked nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig sign, and a non-blanching petechial rash on his trunk and lower limbs. A CT brain performed before lumbar puncture shows no contraindication to LP. CSF is being collected.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 C, HR 116 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 22, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6).
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: no mass lesion, no midline shift, no hydrocephalus. CRP 268 mg/L, WCC 19.8 x10^9/L. CSF pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antimicrobial therapy to commence immediately?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g twelve-hourly with IV dexamethasone 10 mg before or with it
options.rationale: For suspected bacterial meningitis in an immunocompetent young adult, IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly is the empiric treatment of choice, covering Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae. IV dexamethasone 10 mg should be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics (continued 6-hourly for 4 days) to reduce mortality and hearing loss, particularly in pneumococcal meningitis. The petechial rash strongly suggests meningococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; IDSA Meningitis Guidelines)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g four-hourly alone without adjunctive corticosteroids
options.rationale: IV benzylpenicillin is an acceptable pre-hospital empiric treatment for suspected meningococcal disease but is not the definitive in-hospital regimen. Ceftriaxone provides broader coverage and better CNS penetration. Omitting dexamethasone misses the proven mortality and morbidity benefit of adjunctive corticosteroids. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg per kg with IV meropenem 2 g eight-hourly combination
options.rationale: Vancomycin plus meropenem is a broader empiric regimen used in healthcare-associated meningitis or when resistant organisms are suspected. In a young immunocompetent patient with community-acquired meningitis, this regimen is unnecessarily broad and omits the important adjunctive dexamethasone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV aciclovir 10 mg per kg eight-hourly alone for suspected viral encephalitis
options.rationale: The clinical presentation with petechial rash, acute onset, and marked inflammatory markers points to bacterial meningitis rather than viral encephalitis. HSV encephalitis typically presents with temporal lobe features, seizures, and personality change rather than a purpuric rash. Aciclovir should not replace empiric bacterial cover in this scenario. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer all antibiotics until CSF results confirm bacterial meningitis on Gram stain
options.rationale: Deferring antibiotics until CSF results are available is dangerous and increases mortality. In suspected bacterial meningitis, empiric antibiotics must be given immediately. If lumbar puncture is delayed for any reason, antibiotics should be given before LP. CSF culture may be positive even after antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; IDSA Meningitis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute bacterial meningitis in an immunocompetent young adult is most commonly caused by Neisseria meningitidis or Streptococcus pneumoniae. The presence of a petechial or purpuric rash is strongly suggestive of meningococcal disease. Empiric treatment is IV ceftriaxone with adjunctive IV dexamethasone, which should be given before or simultaneously with the first antibiotic dose. Dexamethasone reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis and hearing loss in meningococcal disease. Antibiotics must never be delayed for investigations.
key_points: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly is empiric first-line for community-acquired bacterial meningitis
IV dexamethasone 10 mg must be given before or with the first antibiotic dose
Petechial rash with meningism is strongly suggestive of meningococcal disease
Never delay antibiotics for LP or imaging results
explanation.pearls: The de Gans trial demonstrated that adjunctive dexamethasone (10 mg IV before first antibiotic dose, then 6-hourly for 4 days) reduced mortality in pneumococcal meningitis from 34% to 14% (RR 0.48) and should be discontinued if CSF culture confirms an organism other than S. pneumoniae, as the benefit is not proven for meningococcal meningitis.

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 woman underwent suction evacuation 2 weeks ago for a complete hydatidiform mole diagnosed at 11 weeks gestation on ultrasound (snowstorm appearance, no foetal parts). Histopathology confirmed complete hydatidiform mole with no evidence of invasion. Her pre-evacuation beta-hCG was 320,000 IU/L. Two weeks post-evacuation, her beta-hCG has fallen to 18,000 IU/L. She is currently asymptomatic with no vaginal bleeding. Chest X-ray is normal. She asks about the follow-up protocol and when she can try to conceive again.

data_table.Investigations: Pre-evacuation beta-hCG: 320,000 IU/L. 2-week post-evacuation beta-hCG: 18,000 IU/L. Histology: complete hydatidiform mole, p57 negative (confirming complete mole). CXR: normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up and contraception advice?

options.text: Monthly beta-hCG until normal then 6 months of surveillance with any contraception
options.rationale: While serial beta-hCG monitoring is correct, the follow-up duration for a complete mole is longer than 6 months after normalisation. Australian and international guidelines recommend continued monthly monitoring for 6-12 months after beta-hCG normalises, not just until normalisation. (Source: RANZCOG GTD Guideline; FIGO GTD Guidelines)

options.text: Weekly beta-hCG until normal then monthly for 12 months with reliable contraception
options.rationale: For complete hydatidiform mole, Australian guidelines recommend weekly beta-hCG until normalisation (< 5 IU/L), then monthly beta-hCG for 12 months after normalisation. Reliable contraception (preferably non-hormonal or progestogen-only until hCG normalises, then any method) is essential to avoid pregnancy which would confound hCG monitoring. Conception should be deferred until the 12-month surveillance period is complete. The risk of gestational trophoblastic neoplasia after complete mole is approximately 15-20%. (Source: RANZCOG GTD Guideline; FIGO GTD Guidelines)

options.text: Prophylactic single-agent methotrexate now to prevent gestational trophoblastic neoplasia
options.rationale: Prophylactic chemotherapy after molar evacuation is not standard practice. The majority of complete moles (80-85%) will resolve spontaneously with surveillance alone. Chemotherapy is reserved for confirmed gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (plateauing or rising beta-hCG, histological evidence of invasion, or metastatic disease). (Source: FIGO GTD Guidelines)

options.text: Single beta-hCG at 6 weeks post-evacuation and if normal no further follow-up
options.rationale: A single follow-up beta-hCG is grossly insufficient for molar pregnancy surveillance. GTN can develop weeks to months after evacuation. Complete moles have a 15-20% risk of persistent or malignant disease requiring ongoing surveillance. A declining hCG at 2 weeks does not exclude later plateau or rise. (Source: RANZCOG GTD Guideline)

options.text: Conception permitted once beta-hCG normalises without any surveillance period after
options.rationale: Conception immediately after hCG normalisation would confound subsequent surveillance and could mask the development of GTN. The 12-month monitoring period after normalisation allows detection of late recurrence, which occurs in approximately 3-5% of cases. A new pregnancy's hCG would be indistinguishable from recurrent disease. (Source: RANZCOG GTD Guideline; FIGO GTD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Complete hydatidiform mole carries a 15-20% risk of developing gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. Post-evacuation surveillance requires weekly serum beta-hCG until normalisation, followed by monthly monitoring for 12 months. Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout the surveillance period to prevent a new pregnancy confounding hCG interpretation. GTN is suspected when hCG plateaus over 3 consecutive weekly values, rises over 2 consecutive values, or remains elevated at 6 months post-evacuation. The cure rate for GTN exceeds 98% with chemotherapy.
key_points: Complete mole: 15-20% risk of GTN; partial mole: 0.5-5% risk
Weekly hCG until normal, then monthly for 12 months (complete) or 6 months (partial)
Reliable contraception throughout the surveillance period is mandatory
Rising or plateauing hCG after molar evacuation requires investigation for GTN
explanation.pearls: The diagnostic criteria for post-molar GTN include: beta-hCG plateau over 4 values across 3 weeks, beta-hCG rise of more than 10% over 3 values across 2 weeks, histological diagnosis of choriocarcinoma, or beta-hCG still elevated at 6 months post-evacuation - any single criterion triggers referral for chemotherapy.

stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to a general practice 72 hours after unprotected sexual intercourse requesting emergency contraception. Her last menstrual period was 14 days ago and her cycles are regular at 28 days. She weighs 58 kg and has no significant medical history. She is not taking any regular medications. She has never used an intrauterine device previously and is interested in the most effective option available. She is not currently planning pregnancy in the next 2 years.
lead_in: What is the most effective emergency contraception option for this patient?

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel 1.5 mg as a single dose taken immediately at this visit
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel EC has declining efficacy after 48 hours, with failure rates of 1-2% when taken within 24 hours rising to approximately 5% by 72 hours. At 72 hours post-intercourse during the likely fertile window, levonorgestrel is the least effective option. The copper IUD is significantly more effective. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Oral ulipristal acetate 30 mg as a single dose taken immediately at this visit
options.rationale: Ulipristal acetate maintains efficacy up to 120 hours and is more effective than levonorgestrel at 72 hours. However, the copper IUD remains the most effective emergency contraception method overall, with failure rates below 0.1% compared to approximately 1-2% for UPA. The patient is also interested in ongoing contraception. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive (Yuzpe method) with two doses 12 hours apart now
options.rationale: The Yuzpe method (high-dose combined OCP) is the least effective emergency contraception method and causes significant nausea and vomiting. It has been largely superseded by levonorgestrel, ulipristal, and the copper IUD. It should only be used if no other options are available. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Copper intrauterine device inserted today as the most effective available option
options.rationale: The copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception method with a failure rate below 0.1%, regardless of time since intercourse (effective up to 5 days after the earliest estimated ovulation or up to 5 days after intercourse). It also provides highly effective ongoing contraception for up to 10 years, aligning with this patient's desire for contraception over the next 2 years. At 72 hours post-intercourse near ovulation, the copper IUD offers substantially superior efficacy compared to oral methods. (Source: FSRH Emergency Contraception Guideline; RANZCOG Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg as a single dose for emergency contraceptive purposes
options.rationale: Mifepristone at low doses has been studied for emergency contraception in some countries but is not approved or available for this indication in Australia. In Australia, mifepristone is only available as part of the medical termination of pregnancy regimen (MS-2 Step). (Source: AMH; TGA)

explanation.summary: Emergency contraception options in Australia include oral levonorgestrel, oral ulipristal acetate, and the copper IUD. The copper IUD is the most effective method with failure rates below 0.1%, effective up to 5 days after intercourse, and provides ongoing long-acting reversible contraception. Ulipristal acetate is more effective than levonorgestrel, particularly between 72 and 120 hours. Levonorgestrel is most effective within 24 hours and has declining efficacy thereafter. The copper IUD should be offered to all women seeking emergency contraception, particularly when ongoing contraception is also desired.
key_points: Copper IUD is the most effective EC method (failure rate < 0.1%)
Copper IUD also provides ongoing LARC for up to 10 years
UPA is more effective than LNG beyond 48 hours
LNG efficacy declines significantly after 48-72 hours
explanation.pearls: Ulipristal acetate is a selective progesterone receptor modulator that can delay ovulation even after the LH surge has begun, whereas levonorgestrel cannot prevent ovulation once the LH surge has started - this mechanistic difference explains UPA's superior efficacy in the late follicular phase.

stem: A 35-year-old previously healthy woman presents with 3 days of fever, headache, and behavioural change. Her partner reports she has been increasingly confused, with word-finding difficulties and episodes of agitation. Today she had a witnessed 90-second tonic-clonic seizure at home. On examination she is febrile, disoriented to time and place, and has a receptive aphasia. There is no rash and no nuchal rigidity. MRI brain shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and swelling in the left temporal lobe extending to the insular cortex. CSF shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 C, HR 98 bpm, BP 134/82 mmHg, GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6).
data_table.Investigations: MRI: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity left temporal lobe and insula with mild mass effect. CSF: WCC 86 cells per microlitre (95% lymphocytes), protein 0.92 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (serum 5.2), RBC 120 per microlitre. CSF HSV PCR: pending (result expected in 24-48 hours).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Await CSF HSV PCR result before starting treatment to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Delaying aciclovir until HSV PCR results return is unacceptable. HSV encephalitis has a mortality exceeding 70% without treatment and approximately 20% with early treatment. PCR results take 24-48 hours, and each hour of delay worsens outcomes. Treatment must begin on clinical suspicion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; Lancet Neurology HSV encephalitis review)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g twelve-hourly with dexamethasone for bacterial meningitis cover
options.rationale: The clinical picture (temporal lobe involvement on MRI, behavioural change, seizure, lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose) is classic for HSV encephalitis, not bacterial meningitis. Bacterial meningitis typically shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, and meningeal signs. While empiric antibiotics may be given until bacterial infection is excluded, aciclovir for HSV encephalitis is the critical treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV aciclovir 10 mg per kg eight-hourly immediately plus levetiracetam for seizures
options.rationale: The combination of fever, behavioural change, temporal lobe MRI changes, seizure, and lymphocytic CSF with mildly elevated protein and RBCs is classic for HSV encephalitis. IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly must be commenced immediately without waiting for PCR confirmation. Treatment duration is typically 14-21 days. Antiepileptic therapy (levetiracetam or valproate) should be commenced given the clinical seizure and temporal lobe inflammation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; IDSA Encephalitis Guidelines)

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 1 g three times daily as equivalent to intravenous aciclovir
options.rationale: Oral valaciclovir does not achieve sufficient CSF concentrations for the treatment of HSV encephalitis. IV aciclovir is mandatory for CNS HSV infection. Oral valaciclovir is appropriate for mucocutaneous herpes and varicella zoster dermatomal disease but not for encephalitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; AMH)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days for suspected autoimmune encephalitis
options.rationale: While autoimmune encephalitis is on the differential for temporal lobe encephalitis, the acute febrile onset with CSF RBCs and MRI showing temporal lobe swelling strongly favours HSV encephalitis. High-dose corticosteroids without antiviral cover in HSV encephalitis would be catastrophic. Autoimmune encephalitis is typically subacute and afebrile. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: HSV encephalitis is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis. It has a predilection for the temporal and frontal lobes, presenting with fever, behavioural change, seizures, and aphasia. MRI showing temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity is highly characteristic. CSF typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with mildly elevated protein, normal or mildly reduced glucose, and occasionally red blood cells reflecting haemorrhagic necrosis. Treatment with IV aciclovir must commence immediately on clinical suspicion without awaiting CSF HSV PCR results.
key_points: Temporal lobe involvement on MRI with fever and behavioural change is classic for HSV encephalitis
IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly must start immediately - do not await PCR
CSF RBCs can be present due to haemorrhagic necrosis of the temporal lobe
Treatment duration is 14-21 days; repeat CSF PCR may guide duration
explanation.pearls: A negative CSF HSV PCR within the first 72 hours of symptom onset does not exclude HSV encephalitis, as PCR sensitivity is approximately 98% but may be falsely negative very early in the disease course; if clinical suspicion remains high, aciclovir should be continued and the LP repeated at 3-7 days.
